message:
type: SURVEY
files: src/jinja2/filters.py
note: Adding with_counts param to groupby filter: touches _GroupTuple class (NamedTuple, may need new tuple/class for count), sync_do_groupby() and async do_groupby() functions

type: SURVEY
files: src/jinja2/filters.py
note: Adding case_sensitive param to groupby(): will edit sync_do_groupby() and async do_groupby() function signatures/bodies (lines ~1160-1230). Not touching make_attrgetter (already supports postprocess param) or _GroupTuple class.

type: DRAFT files: src/jinja2/filters.py region: sync_do_groupby function code: @pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will have the case of the first item in that group of values. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" group will have two values. This can be disabled by passing ``case_sensitive=True``. If ``with_counts`` is enabled, each group is a ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuple containing the number of items in the group, instead of the default ``(grouper, list)`` tuple. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ) out = [ (key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] if with_counts: return [(key, values, len(values)) for key, values in out] return [_GroupTuple(key, values) for key, values in out] note: Merged sync_do_groupby with both case_sensitive and with_counts params. with_counts returns plain 3-tuples (grouper, list, count) instead of _GroupTuple when enabled, to avoid changing the namedtuple shape for the default case.

type: DRAFT files: src/jinja2/filters.py region: sync_do_groupby function code: @pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will have the case of the first item in that group of values. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" group will have two values. This can be disabled by passing ``case_sensitive=True``. If ``with_counts`` is enabled, each group is a ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuple containing the number of items in the group, instead of the default ``(grouper, list)`` tuple. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ) out = [ (key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] if not case_sensitive: # Return the real key from the first value instead of the lowercase key. original_expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = [(original_expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in out] if with_counts: return [(key, values, len(values)) for key, values in out] return [_GroupTuple(key, values) for key, values in out] note: Fix vs your last draft: your version returned the lowercased key directly from groupby(), which loses original casing (e.g. always outputs 'ca' not 'CA'). Added a second pass that re-derives the grouper from the first item's real (non-lowercased) attribute value when case_sensitive=False, so 'CA' wins over 'ca' if it appears first, matching sort()'s case-insensitive convention. with_counts logic otherwise unchanged from your draft.

Now drafting async do_groupby with the same case_sensitive + with_counts logic, will send next.

type: DRAFT
files: src/jinja2/filters.py
region: async do_groupby function
code: @async_variant(sync_do_groupby)  # type: ignore
async def do_groupby(
    environment: "Environment",
    value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]",
    attribute: t.Union[str, int],
    default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None,
    case_sensitive: bool = False,
    with_counts: bool = False,
) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]":
    expr = make_attrgetter(
        environment,
        attribute,
        postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None,
        default=default,
    )
    out = [
        (key, list(values))
        for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr)
    ]

    if not case_sensitive:
        # Return the real key from the first value instead of the lowercase key.
        original_expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
        out = [(original_expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in out]

    if with_counts:
        return [(key, values, len(values)) for key, values in out]

    return [_GroupTuple(key, values) for key, values in out]
note: Async counterpart mirroring the agreed sync_do_groupby body, using await auto_to_list(value) for the sort() call same as original.

type: AGREE
files: src/jinja2/filters.py
region: async do_groupby function
code: @async_variant(sync_do_groupby)  # type: ignore
async def do_groupby(
    environment: "Environment",
    value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]",
    attribute: t.Union[str, int],
    default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None,
    case_sensitive: bool = False,
    with_counts: bool = False,
) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]":
    expr = make_attrgetter(
        environment,
        attribute,
        postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None,
        default=default,
    )
    out = [
        (key, list(values))
        for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr)
    ]

    if not case_sensitive:
        # Return the real key from the first value instead of the lowercase key.
        original_expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
        out = [(original_expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in out]

    if with_counts:
        return [(key, values, len(values)) for key, values in out]

    return [_GroupTuple(key, values) for key, values in out]
note: Agreed verbatim, will implement exactly as-is. All overlapping constructs (_GroupTuple, sync_do_groupby, do_groupby) now agreed.

type: DONE
files: src/jinja2/filters.py
note: Implemented the agreed merged _GroupTuple, sync_do_groupby, and async do_groupby verbatim. Verified manually: default case-insensitive grouping preserves first-seen casing (CA groups with ca under 'CA'), case_sensitive=True keeps them separate, with_counts=True yields 3-tuples. Existing groupby tests in tests/test_filters.py pass (4 passed).

type: DONE
files: src/jinja2/filters.py
note: Implemented agreed sync_do_groupby, do_groupby, and _GroupTuple verbatim. Ran existing groupby tests (pass) and manual checks combining case_sensitive + with_counts together (pass). Writing final patch now.
